content: Hi. When I write some drivers in rust, I need to use something similar to "for_each_online_cpu(CPU)" C macro. But I do not find anything proper in the rust-for-linux. Are there some ways for me to achieve this goal? Thanks for your time in advance.

I try to use a function in helper.c to wrap this macro. But I find it hard to implement a function that has the same ability as this macro. Because this macro wraps some statements inside rather than acts as a part of some statement.

I am currently thinking about writing a similar but not the same wrapper function, but I don't know whether it is correct. Rather than returning a CPU index at a time, this wrapper function gives a list of CPU indexes at once, which can be passed to something like "per_cpu" C macro one by one.
Is it possible to invent something like for_each_online_cpu(f: FnOnce)? Just thinking out loud here.
I think you are correct that you cannot simply reuse the C macro unfortunately, it will need to be re-implemented.
Or write a wrapper around it in C that calls a specified callback function.

Wei Liu said:

Is it possible to invent something like for_each_online_cpu(f: FnOnce)? Just thinking out loud here.

Will this function rely on the C macros or just base on the rust side?
I suppose it still needs to call into that macro at some point, so this doesn't really solve your issue.

Alex Gaynor said:

I think you are correct that you cannot simply reuse the C macro unfortunately, it will need to be re-implemented.

In this case, will we re-implement other macros like per_cpu or DEFINE_PER_CPU totally from rust considering they are showing at the same time?

Wei Liu said:

I suppose it still needs to call into that macro at some point, so this doesn't really solve your issue.

It seems impossible to implement this in a function with same function. I will try to write a wrapper function return a online CPU list. Thanks for your help!

bjorn3 said:

Or write a wrapper around it in C that calls a specified callback function.

Thanks for your advice! I will go to see whether this can work.
In terms of ergonomics, I think we need to implement an iterator that returns all the cpu indices, then we would use the regular Rust idioms for this, i.e., for cpu in kernel::online_cpus() { ... }
If it is written as an iterator, it looks easier to understand.
I wonder should this iterator be implemented totally in rust or it's ok to use some C macros.

We definitely don't need nor do we want the for_each_* macros (we want first-class iterators). We may need to use some C macros or C inline functions (for which we'll need to add helpers in helpers.c) to implement the iterators; from a quick look now, it looks like cpumask_next is what we need (and it's a regular function, so no need for helpers, yay!)
I will have a try. Thanks for your help!
